items.content:
Introduction The demand for IPTV (Internet Protocol Television) is skyrocketing in the Netherlands, with viewers ditching traditional cable for flexible, internet-based streaming. As a developer, building a scalable and reliable IPTV service isn’t just about delivering content—it’s about optimizing latency, ensuring security, and adapting to regional trends (like the Dutch preference for local sports and news).

In this post, I’ll walk through the technical challenges of building an IPTV service, share code snippets for key functionalities, and explain how platforms like IPTV Totaal are solving these problems for Dutch users.

  1. The Architecture of a Modern IPTV Service IPTV relies on a robust backend to handle video streaming, user authentication, and real-time updates. Here’s a simplified architecture:

Content Delivery Network (CDN): Distribute video streams efficiently across the Netherlands.

Middleware: Handle user logins, subscriptions, and channel management.

Player: A lightweight, cross-platform player (e.g., HLS.js or Video.js).

Example: Token-Based Authentication in Node.js `// Generate a JWT token for user sessions

const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');

function generateToken(user) {

{ userId: user.id, plan: user.subscriptionPlan },

  1. Overcoming Latency Challenges in the Netherlands Dutch viewers expect instant access to live TV, especially for events like Eredivisie football matches. To reduce buffering:

Use WebRTC for low-latency live streaming.

Deploy edge servers in Amsterdam (AWS or Cloudflare).

Optimize video compression with FFmpeg:# Convert video to HLS format with adaptive bitrate ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -c:v h264 -crf 22 -preset fast \

-hls_time 4 -hls_playlist_type event \

output.m3u8 `

  1. Securing Your IPTV Service Piracy is a major concern. Here’s how to protect your streams:

AES Encryption: Encrypt HLS segments.

Geo-Blocking: Restrict access to Dutch IPs using NGINX:location /live { if ($allowed_country != NL) { }

proxy_pass http://backend;

}

  1. The Dutch Edge: Localization Matters To succeed in the Netherlands:

Integrate Local Content: Partner with Dutch channels (e.g., NPO, RTL).

Accept iDEAL Payments: Use APIs like Mollie for seamless Dutch transactions.

Comply with EU Regulations: GDPR and the AVMS Directive.

  1. Why IPTV Totaal Stands Out At IPTV Totaal, we’ve tackled these challenges head-on to offer:

Ultra-low latency streams for Dutch sports and news.

Secure, GDPR-compliant subscriptions with iDEAL support.

24/7 customer support in Dutch.

Whether you’re a developer building your own service or a viewer looking for reliable streaming, the future of TV is internet-driven—and the Netherlands is leading the charge.

🔍 Curious about IPTV solutions tailored for the Netherlands? Explore IPTV Totaal for developer-friendly APIs or seamless streaming experiences.

items.title: Interview Cheat Sheet: App Performance in React Native
items.author: Rushi Patel
items.content:
Special Article: Interview Cheat Sheet

This cheat sheet is designed to help you quickly answer interview questions and prepare for presentations on optimizing app performance in React Native. It covers the need for performance optimization, detailed app-level and code-level strategies, what React Native offers out of the box, and common mistakes to avoid.

Why Focus on App Performance in React Native?

    What this cheat sheet covers:

    • Strategies that affect the overall app architecture and resource management.
    • Code-Level Optimizations: Best practices in coding to reduce unnecessary computations and improve rendering.
    • Built-in tools and libraries that enhance performance.

    • Efficient Asset Management:

      • Use appropriately sized images and leverage caching tools.
      • Utilize SVGs for vector images and host images on a CDN to reduce bundle size.
      • Implement lazy loading to defer non-critical assets until needed.
      • Combine multiple network requests into one.
      • Use caching strategies to reduce redundant calls.

    • Optimized Build Configuration:

      • Load only the necessary code chunks.
      • Remove unused code with modern bundlers.
      • Minification & Obfuscation: Use production builds to reduce size and improve load times.
      • Integrate tools like to monitor performance.
      • Employ error tracking services (e.g., Sentry) to catch runtime issues early.
      • Avoid Unnecessary Renders: Use , , and control re-renders.
      • Leverage and to memoize values and functions.

    • Avoid Inline Functions & Objects:

      • Define functions and objects outside of render or memoize them to prevent re-creation on each render.

    • Efficient State Management:

      • Keep state localized when possible.
      • Use immutable updates to make re-render decisions more efficient.
      • Always set to offload animation work to native code.
      • This is transfer the heavy animation operations from JS to Native side (Android/iOS)

    • Avoid Expensive Computations in Render:

      • Precompute heavy values outside the render cycle.
      • Reuse the heavy computed values instead of calculating them every single time.
      • Use , , or to render large datasets efficiently.
      • Avoid using when rendering homogeneous large data and instead leverage the Virtualization.

    • Effective use of useEffect:

      • Manage side effects properly. For example, fetch data on component mount:
      • Verify the dependency array is correct to avoid unnecessary trigger of useEffects.
      • There is no thumb rule, but try to avoid using too many useEffects in a single component.
      • Implement error boundaries to catch and handle runtime errors gracefully.
      • This improves the User Experience (UX) as app doesn't crashes for runtime errors.

    • Leveraging Async Operations:

      • Use asynchronous functions () to offload heavy computations and avoid blocking the main thread.

    • Advanced Network Optimizations:

      • Implement request batching, pagination, and caching responses at the code level to reduce data load and improve performance.

    What React Native Provides for a Performant App (Brief)

    • Hermes JavaScript Engine: Improves start-up time and reduces memory usage.
    • Native Driver for Animations: Offloads animations to native code.
    • Provides insights into network calls, layout performance, and more.
    • Enhances developer experience by retaining component state during updates.
    • Efficient communication between JavaScript and native modules. (part of Old Architecture)
    • Components like , , and are designed for high performance.
    • Use tools like CodePush to deliver performance improvements without a full app update.

    Pro-tip: Leveraging tools like , , and

    Using these tools can significantly improve code quality while writing it. These tools help enforce best practices, catch potential bugs early, and ensure consistency in the codebase.

    Integrating them into your development workflow enables real-time feedback, reducing technical debt and improving maintainability.

    This cheat sheet serves as a handy reference to quickly recall essential strategies and best practices when discussing or implementing performance optimizations in React Native. Use it to prepare for interviews, technical discussions, or presentations, and ensure you cover both high-level concepts and practical code-level techniques.

    For any questions or suggestions, please feel free to comment below. 💬

    If you find this article useful, share it with your friends and follow for regular update of such good articles. 🔗

    Happy interviewing and coding!

    Rushi Patel, Signing Off! 😊

items.title: Managing Persistence and Business Logic in Spring: A Clean and Scalable Approach
items.author: Thomas
items.content:
When it comes to managing data access in Java applications, balancing between clean business logic and efficient persistence can be tricky. One of the most effective ways to achieve this balance in a Spring-based application is by separating your domain logic from your persistence layer. By utilizing the right tools, you can ensure that your code is both maintainable and scalable.

In this post, I’ll walk you through an approach that leverages Spring Data JPA, custom repository implementations, and Specifications to provide clean, flexible data access while keeping business logic separate.

The Basic Structure: Domain Objects vs. Entities At the heart of our approach is the distinction between domain objects and entities.

Domain Objects represent the business logic of your application. These are the objects your services, controllers, and other application layers will interact with. Entities, on the other hand, are the objects that interact directly with the database. They’re tied to your JPA annotations and represent the structure of the database tables.

For example, we might have a Book domain object and a BookEntity that maps to a book table in the database. The goal is to never expose entities directly in your business logic. Instead, you transform them into domain objects that contain only the necessary business data.

Step 1: Define the Repository Interface (Domain Layer)

Start by creating an interface that defines your domain-specific operations. Here’s an example for a Book repository:

public interface Books { List<Book> findAll(); Book findById(Long id);

void deleteById(Long id);

List<Book> findByTitle(String title); Notice that we’re only working with Book domain objects here. There’s no mention of BookEntity or the database at this stage.

Step 2: Implement the Repository (Persistence Layer)

Next, we create a custom repository implementation. This implementation will use Spring Data JPA’s JpaRepository to interact with the database. The repository implementation will map between domain objects and entities as needed.

`@Repository public class BookRepositoryImpl implements Books {

private final BookRepository repository;

protected BookRepositoryImpl(BookRepository repository) {
    this.repository = repository;
}

@Override
public List<Book> findAll() {
    return this.repository.findAll().stream()
            .map(BookEntity::toDomain)  // Convert Entity to Domain object
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

@Override
public Book save(Book book) {
    BookEntity saved = this.repository.save(BookEntity.create(book.getTitle(), book.getPublishedDate()));
    return saved.toDomain();
}

@Override
public Book findById(Long id) {
    return this.repository.findById(id).map(BookEntity::toDomain)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new BookNotFoundException("Book not found with id: " + id));
}

@Override
public void deleteById(Long id) {
    this.repository.deleteById(id);
}

@Override
public List<Book> findByTitle(String title) {
    Specification<BookEntity> hasTitle = BookSpecification.hasTitle(title);
    return this.repository.findAll(Specification.where(hasTitle)).stream()
            .map(BookEntity::toDomain)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

}` Key Points:

BookEntity to Domain Conversion: Each method in the implementation maps entities to domain objects using the toDomain() method.

Custom Query Handling: The findByTitle method demonstrates how you can use a Specification to dynamically build queries without hardcoding them into your repository methods.

Step 3: Extend JpaRepository for Basic CRUD Operations

We don’t have to manually implement basic CRUD operations. By extending JpaRepository, we can let Spring Data JPA handle the heavy lifting for us. This gives us methods like findAll(), save(), findById(), etc., for free.

public interface BookRepository extends JpaRepository<BookEntity, Long>, JpaSpecificationExecutor<BookEntity> { }

By extending JpaSpecificationExecutor, you also get the ability to run dynamic queries using Specification objects—more on this in a moment.

Step 4: Working with Specifications

Specifications are part of the JPA Criteria API and allow us to build complex queries in a clean, reusable, and composable way. This is especially useful when you need to write dynamic queries based on various search criteria.

For example, let’s create a simple specification to search for books by title:

public class BookSpecification { static Specification<BookEntity> hasTitle(String title) {

return (root, query, cb) -> cb.like(root.get("title"), "%" + title + "%");

} The beauty of specifications is that you can easily combine multiple conditions to create more complex queries without cluttering up your repository layer. You can also add more methods to BookSpecification for other conditions like author, published date, etc.

Step 5: Combining Specifications and Query Execution

Now, let’s see how we combine the specification in the repository implementation:

@Override public List<Book> findByTitle(String title) {

Specification<BookEntity> hasTitle = BookSpecification.hasTitle(title);

return this.repository.findAll(Specification.where(hasTitle)).stream()

.map(BookEntity::toDomain)

.collect(Collectors.toList()); Here, we’re using the Specification.where() method to apply our title filter and executing the query using findAll(). This approach allows us to compose complex query conditions in a clean and flexible manner.

Step 6: Error Handling and Best Practices

Error handling is an important aspect of any repository layer. In our case, we’re using orElseThrow() to throw an exception when a book is not found by its ID. You might want to create a custom exception (e.g., BookNotFoundException) to provide more context or return a custom error message:

@Override public Book findById(Long id) {

return this.repository.findById(id)

.map(BookEntity::toDomain)

.orElseThrow(() -> new BookNotFoundException("Book not found with id: " + id));

}

By separating the domain objects from the persistence layer, we can keep our business logic clean and decoupled from database concerns. The repository pattern, combined with Spring Data JPA’s JpaRepository and JpaSpecificationExecutor, allows us to manage data access in a flexible and maintainable way.

The use of Specifications is a powerful tool for writing dynamic, complex queries while maintaining clean and reusable code. With this approach, your data access layer becomes both efficient and scalable, helping you to easily adapt to changing business requirements.

By following these principles, you’ll be able to create a robust and maintainable Spring application with a clear separation between business logic and persistence concerns.

items.title: Ternary Operators in JavaScript: Dead or Still Useful?
items.author: shelly agarwal
items.content:
As a senior developer of 5+ years and working on multiple projects, I have seen many developers use ternary operators on a daily basis, but is it worth using every single time? 🤔

I am quite comfortable using it to be honest, it is really simple to use and easy to write but there are many cases when we must avoid it in order to avoid bugs in a complex condition.

There is only 1 condition.

const flashStatus = `Flash ${buttonType === 'flash' ? "on" : "off"}`

☝️

If multiple conditions are present.

const shutterCallBack =
  selectedOption === 'Video' ? (isRecording ? stopRecordingVideo : recordVideo) : takePhoto;

☝️

  1. If any new team member joins the team, they won't know what the condition is doing and they can actually break the code by adding more conditions if they don't understand it in the first place.
  2. It is really hard to debug the condition if any issue occurs because you need to identify each condition output before you make any changes.
  3. Maintainability Issues of course.

What is the solution then? 💡

  1. Keep your conditions simple and easy to understand.
  2. Use either switch statement or if/else in order to maintain these dependent conditions
  3. Add necessary comments to provide context what these conditions are doing.

You can avoid nested ternary operators and change the condition like this -> ✔️ If-Else Statements (More Readable)

let shutterCallBack;

if (selectedOption === 'Video') {
  if (isRecording) {
    shutterCallBack = stopRecordingVideo;
  } else {
    shutterCallBack = recordVideo;
  }
} else {
  shutterCallBack = takePhoto;
}

✔️ Object Mapping (When Dealing with Categories)

const shutterActions = {
  Video: isRecording ? stopRecordingVideo : recordVideo,
  Photo: takePhoto,
};

const shutterCallBack = shutterActions[selectedOption] || takePhoto;

Ternary operators are obviously not dead but you need to make sure use these only for precise conditions and not on multiple or complex conditions.

What do you think? Comment your thoughts..

items.title: How to Create a VM Data Disk, Initialize it and make it usable in Microsoft Azure
items.author: Tawo Wilmer Busa
items.content:
An Azure VM Data Disk is a managed disk that provides additional storage for a Virtual Machine (VM) in Microsoft Azure. It is separate from the OS disk and is used to store application data, databases and other files.

In this article, we will be looking at step-by-step guidelines on how to add data disk and initialize it to make it usable on Azure VM.

Guideline on How to Add a Data Disk to a Windows Virtual Machine through the Azure PortalStep 1: Create and Attach a Data Disk

Search for Virtual Machine in the search bar and select

  • Click on "Virtual Machines" and select the VM where you want to add a data disk
  • Add or create a new resource group
  • Choose a virtual machine name, region and availability zone
  • Choose the image (OS) and size of the virtual machine
  • Create a username and password

Public inbound ports: click on Allow selected ports Select inbound ports: select HTTP (80)

Click on tick mark(✓) Click on Next:Disks >

  • Go to the Monitoring to disable the boot diagnostic

Give it a name and value

  • Go to Review + create_ and click create
  • Click on the Go to Resource
  • On the overview go to settings
  • Click settings, then select disks
  • On Data disks page, click on + create and attach new disk
  • On the LUN: Choose disk name, storage type, and size
  • Click on select and wait for the validation till it is configured

  • Click on Download RDP file

  • Connect to the VM: Use Remote Desktop (RDP) to connect to your Windows VM.

  • Open Disk Management: Press Win + X and select Disk Management (or type diskmgmt.msc in the search box and press Enter)

  • On Disk Management Click on create and format hard disk partitions

  • Right-click on the unallocated space of the new disk. Select New Simple Volume
  • Follow the wizard to assign a drive letter and format the disk

This will be the end of our learning on Understanding how to attach new data disks to a Windows virtual machine through the Azure portal. With Azure, you can easily expand your VM’s capacity by adding more data disks or resizing existing ones to meet the demands of your workloads. I hope you had an informative learning journey on this topic.

items.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Azure Compute Gallery and Capturing a VM Image
items.author: Kosisochukwu Ugochukwu
items.content:
Azure Compute Gallery (formerly Shared Image Gallery) enables customers to manage, share, and replicate VM images across regions. This guide describes how to capture an image of a VM, configure Azure Compute Gallery, and deploy VMs using the image that was captured.

Step 1: Create an Azure Compute Gallery

  • : Navigate to Azure Portal and sign in.

Navigate to Compute Galleries:

  • Search for "Azure Compute Gallery" in the Azure Portal.
  • Enter a name for the gallery and region.
  • Click "Review + Create" → "Create".
  • Wait for to finish
  • Azure compute gallery created: Now we can create and deploy custom images.

Step 2: Capture an Image of the VM (Specialized State)Select the Virtual Machine:

  • Go to "Virtual Machines" and choose the VM you want to capture.
  • Ensure the VM is in a and state otherwise the VM will automatically stop while in the process of deployment.
  • In the VM overview page, click .
  • Choose Capture an image of this VM.
  • Under "OS state", select (since the VM is not generalized).
  • Azure Compute Gallery: Choose we created as the target destination.
  • Create an Image Definition
  • Click "Create" → "Image Definition"
  • Enter a name and choose the operating system.
  • Select the VM generation (Gen1 or Gen2).
  • Leave everything else as default and click .
  • Choose "Specialized" under "OS State".
  • Note that it inheriting of the properties of the VM created earlier
  • Version number: Choose 0.0.1
  • End of life: choose any date you prefer
  • Define replication regions (optional for redundancy).
  • Click "Review + Create" → "Create".
  • Notice the VM being stopped as the validation is in progress

Notice we can use a captured image on compute gallery to create a VM and a VMSS (virtual machine scale set)

I wish to further show you something very interesting

  • Scroll down to image and notice that the one we just created is listed among the availiable images

By completing these steps, you have successfully created an Azure Compute Gallery, captured a VM image, and deployed new VMs from it. This technology allows for effective management and distribution of virtual machine images while keeping user setups and settings.

items.title: Cloudingo Review: Top Salesforce Data Cleansing Tool
items.author: Dorian Sabitov
items.content:
Introduction: Clean Up Your Data Garden

Managing customer data in Salesforce can sometimes feel like walking through a wild garden. Records often get duplicated, fields might be incomplete, and inconsistent information can lead you to a dead end.

When you can’t be sure that your data is trustworthy, you risk inaccurate reporting, confusing team members, and disappointing customers. And this is not the result you expect from your investment in a CRM system, especially at a time when AI-ready data is becoming an essential need. Any company working or planning to work with AI should have clean data. And it’s not a fancy thing right now, it’s a necessity today to integrate your business with AI if you want to be one step ahead of your competitors.

Additionally, Gartner predicts that through 2026, organizations will abandon 60% of AI projects unsupported by AI-ready data.

A proper Salesforce data cleaning tool, in these scenarios, can be the reliable assistant that helps you clean up your data garden and restore confidence in your CRM.

  • But What is the Cloudingo App, Exactly?
  • Where to Start: Installing and Configuring Cloudingo
    • Step #1. Visit the AppExchange
    • Step #2. Start Installation Process
    • Step #3. Confirm Installation Details
    • Step #4. Post-Installation
    • Step #5. Create a Cloudingo Account
    • Step #6. Connect Cloudingo With Salesforce
  • Key Features and Functionalities of Cloudingo
    • Feature #1. Duplicate Detection
    • Feature #2. Merge & Convert
    • Feature #3. Undo/Restore Merges and Conversions
    • Feature #4. Automation & Scheduling
    • Feature #5. Real-Time Processing for Standard Objects
    • Feature #6. Data Quality Dashboard
    • Feature #7. Reports and Analytics
    • Feature #8. Field Analysis
    • Feature #10. Data Import Tool
    • Feature #11. Update and Delete Records Functionality
    • Feature #12. Multi-User Capabilities
    • Feature #13. Integration Options
    • Feature #14. Robust Security
  • Pros and Cons About Cloudingo
  • Customer Feedback About Cloudingo

In this article, we will take a look at Cloudingo, a top AppExchange app that can be your savior in the fight against inconsistent data in your Salesforce instance.

But What is the Cloudingo App, Exactly?

At first glance, Cloudingo is an app designed to identify, merge, and remove duplicate data in Salesforce, helping to improve data quality and ensuring that your Salesforce instance is the single source of truth for your records, but it’s even more, so let’s dive into the Cloudingo world!

Sure, the core challenge Cloudingo solves is data duplication. Duplicates can occur because of imports from third-party systems, manual data entry, or legacy processes that never got cleaned up. It’s also easy to miss minor variations in company or contact names. Cloudingo steps in as a Salesforce dedupe tool, ensuring everything merges neatly while preserving important details.

But if you take a closer look, you’ll find that this app has a lot of powerful surprises under the hood. Cloudingo offers features and services that will make data management easier and make your data cleaning efforts much more efficient, like the Data Quality Dashboard, or Personal onboarding and Customer success manager assistance.

The main purpose of Cloudingo is to let you clean and maintain quality data with minimal hassle. Cloudingo leads to clean data, and clean data leads to happy customers, so the main result you can achieve with Cloudingo is your happy customers, and it’s worth trying! The app uses a variety of matching rules and filters, so you can customize how you want to catch duplicates. Plus, it offers automated features, so you don’t have to keep pressing buttons each time you want to clean up Salesforce.

Cloudingo is like a magic wand for Salesforce administrators, data managers, sales teams, marketing professionals, and anyone else who relies heavily on customer data within Salesforce. If you have thousands (or millions) of records and no clear process to manage duplicates or outdated entries, Cloudingo can be a game-changer. It automates the routine work, ensuring your system has only correct and current information.

Organizations that rely on Salesforce can save time, reduce errors, and enhance the reliability of their reports by using this tool.

Where to Start: Installing and Configuring Cloudingo

Installing Cloudingo in your Salesforce Org is straightforward if you follow the official guidelines. Below is a quick outline of how to get started:

Step #1. Visit the AppExchange

First, navigate to the Cloudingo AppExchange page. You’ll see application details, including Cloudingo reviews, pricing, and feature highlights.

Step #2. Start Installation Process

Click “Get It Now” or “Try It Free” depending on the type of organization, Production or Sandbox, in which you want to install the Cloudingo agent, one half of the setup process. You’ll be prompted to log in to your Salesforce account if you’re not already.

Step #3. Confirm Installation Details

Review the package details and decide if you want it for admins only or all users in your Org. Note: Admins Only access is recommended. Users do not work directly with the Cloudingo agent that is installed in Salesforce.

Step #4. Post-Installation

The installation of Cloudingo in your Salesforce organization is complete. You can now find the app among the apps installed in your organization.

Step #5. Create a Cloudingo Account

Once the Cloudingo app has been installed in your Salesforce organization, it’s time to create an account on the Cloudingo website. Open the Cloudingo app and click the “Get Started” button.

On the Cloudingo authorization page, you can log in with your Salesforce credentials, access your existing Cloudingo account, or create a new Cloudingo account.

Step #6. Connect Cloudingo With Salesforce

Once a Cloudingo account is created, you’ll need to connect it to your Salesforce Org. Just enter your Salesforce credentials in the Cloudingo app. Ensure you choose the correct settings and authorize the necessary permissions so the tool can read and write data.

That’s all you need to do to set up Cloudingo. If you want more detailed instructions, Cloudingo has aGetting Started Guide that walks you through each step and offers troubleshooting tips.

Your Cloudingo account is now set up and starts working for you. Let’s see what it can do.

Key Features and Functionalities of Cloudingo

Feature #1. Duplicate Detection

Cloudingo serves as a highly flexible dedupe tool listed on the Salesforce AppExchange, with advanced matching criteria for fields like name, email, or phone. Users can use over a dozen matching styles, including exact, partial, or fuzzy matching, to catch variations such as nicknames or alternate data formats. Filters can be set up for standard or custom objects, ensuring full coverage of leads, contacts, and accounts. Real-time scanning flags potential duplicates immediately, reducing manual cleanups and ensuring data remains consistent across various input channels.

Feature #2. Merge & Convert

Once duplicates are found, Cloudingo merges them into one record while preserving key data from the duplicate set. You can decide which fields should take priority, and you can even convert leads to accounts or contacts on the fly. The merging process re-parents related objects like tasks and opportunities, so no vital information is lost. Whether you merge records one by one, in small batches, or handle mass merges, the process remains transparent, giving you control over how final records are created.

Feature #3. Undo/Restore Merges and Conversions

Cloudingo provides an extra layer of safety with the Undo/Restore feature. If a merge or lead conversion results in lost or conflicting information, admins can revert those changes, restoring previous records and preserving historical data. This rollback capability makes it safer to run automated merges.

Feature #4. Automation & Scheduling

Tired of manually clicking buttons? Cloudingo can schedule cleanup jobs to run automatically thanks to built-in scheduling features. You can set Cloudingo to run deduplication tasks overnight, weekly, or monthly. With Cloudingo, Salesforce administrators are saved from manual oversight. Automated merges follow the rules you define, so conflicts are resolved consistently, and you wake up to a clean Salesforce environment each morning. Scheduling helps large organizations keep pace with constant data entry, import processes, and integrations.

Feature #5. Real-Time Processing for Standard Objects

With Cloudingo, you can detect and merge duplicates of Leads, Contacts, and Accounts as they enter the system in real-time. When new records enter Salesforce, the system checks for potential duplicates. This instant approach prevents duplicates from piling up, maintaining data integrity around the clock.

Feature #6. Data Quality Dashboard

An additional value Cloudingo brings that goes beyond the standard deduplication functionality is the ability to analyze data quality. Cloudingo offers a comprehensive Data Quality Dashboard that shows you the overall health of your Salesforce data. On this dashboard, you can see the overall Data Quality Score, which is calculated through multiple data metrics, or Top Issues/Actions, which are worth paying attention to. The Data Quality Dashboard also shows how many duplicates you have, field completeness statistics, and other indicators that will allow you to see the quality of your data in one place and track these metrics over time. Make your work with data as simple as possible with Cloudingo, open a dashboard, check the data on duplicates, automate the data deduplication, and that’s it! Experience how simple it can be with Cloudingo.

Feature #7. Reports and Analytics

Another data cleaning analytics tool is built-in reports. There are many different types of reports available that allow you to drill down into your data cleaning process, for instance, these reports summarize merges or deletions and let you see exactly which fields were updated. And the customizable Report Dashboard allows you to track the parameters that matter to you at a glance. This transparent audit trail ensures administrators always maintain full control over data changes.

Feature #8. Field Analysis

For deeper insights, the Field Analysis feature analyzes individual fields on the Salesforce data objects to identify potential inconsistencies or incomplete data. Admins can spot outdated, missing, or incorrectly formatted fields quickly, helping teams focus on the most critical areas of data improvement. By highlighting field-level issues, Cloudingo offers a more complex approach to data quality management.

Cloudingo’s “Find Data” feature enables users to upload a CSV file to search for matching records within Salesforce and pull back additional data points from Salesforce into the file. This tool is especially useful for verifying whether certain records already exist in Salesforce before importing or updating data. Users can define match criteria based on Salesforce objects and fields and export search results as a CSV file for further processing. Additionally, saved templates allow for faster execution of repeated searches, making it easier to maintain clean and correct data. That means that you can automate your data searches and the next data search will be done much faster.

Feature #10. Data Import Tool

The Cloudingo Data Import tool makes loading external files into Salesforce smoother by applying Cloudingo’s deduplication logic during import. Users can map CSV columns to Salesforce fields and apply matching rules on the fly. This process removes potential duplicates at the point of entry, keeping records clean without relying on post-import cleanup. Just map fields from the file to Salesforce fields and forget about the data with possible duplicates after each data import.

Feature #11. Update and Delete Records Functionality

Cloudingo also supports broader data maintenance, including updating and deleting records in bulk. Admins can perform mass updates on select fields for an entire batch of records, useful for reclassifying leads or adjusting account attributes. Bulk deletion targets outdated or irrelevant entries, helping keep the Salesforce org uncluttered. With these functions, Cloudingo extends its capabilities beyond simply merging duplicates, offering an advanced toolkit for ongoing data hygiene.

Feature #12. Multi-User Capabilities

Cloudingo lets you add multiple users with different access levels. Admins can define user roles and assign permissions, ensuring that data cleansing tasks are distributed effectively across a team. This feature enables collaboration on filter creation, deduplication jobs, and audit reviews. Combined with Cloudingo’s dashboard, multi-user functionality promotes a streamlined, shared workflow. Working alone or with your teammates, Cloudingo will make working with data easy and efficient.

Feature #13. Integration Options

If your organization pulls data from external systems like ERP, billing, accounting, or marketing tools, you’ll probably love Cloudingo’s handy integration features:

  • Cloudingo can scan data entering Salesforce from multiple external systems, including ERP solutions, accounting platforms, or marketing tools like Marketo. Incoming data is immediately checked using predefined deduplication rules, ensuring your Salesforce environment remains clean, consistent, and free of duplicates across multiple data streams.
  • Specifically for Marketo users, Cloudingo offers direct integration. Just as Cloudingo cleans Salesforce data, it also efficiently scans, identifies, and manages duplicate records within Marketo itself, ensuring consistent data quality.

This combined functionality is tailored to maintaining high data quality across all your core business applications. One, two, or even more platforms, Cloudingo will keep your data clean.

Feature #14. Robust Security

Working with data is always about security. Cloudingo is trusted by over 22,000 businesses worldwide for secure data management. As a certified Salesforce ISV and SOC 2 Type II compliant company, Cloudingo meets Salesforce’s strict security requirements and undergoes annual audits and third-party penetration testing. The Cloudingo agent operates within Salesforce, using real-time API calls instead of storing customer data on external servers (except for the Undo/Restore options, the activation of which requires a separate configuration of the encrypted backup storage location). All interactions are encrypted with 256-bit SSL, and server firewalls ensure protection. By implementing these security measures, Cloudingo ensures compliance, transparency, and secure CRM data handling.

Pros and Cons About Cloudingo

In the previous section, we’ve looked at Cloudingo’s key features and functionality, so now let’s take a balanced look at the strengths and weaknesses of this product.

  • : The dashboard is intuitive, making it easy to set up custom matching rules.
  • : With a variety of matching rules and filters, users have full control over how duplicates are identified and merged, ensuring precise data management tailored to their specific needs.
  • : Scheduling merges saves time and prevents you from having to remember manual cleanup.
  • : See exactly which records merged, how they merged, and any conflicts that arose.
  • : Clean Leads, Contacts, Accounts, and even custom objects.
  • : With Cloudingo’s User Support, which can include Personal onboarding or Customer success manager assistance, even beginners can utilize the full power of Cloudingo to ensure data cleanliness. Also, don’t forget about comprehensive documentation and a video library on Youtube, where you will always find help.
  • Additional Cost for Full Functionality : Some advanced features come with the most expensive subscription, but the time saved and the improved data accuracy make it a worthwhile investment in the long run.

Overall, the strengths tend to outweigh the weaknesses, especially for organizations that are serious about clean Salesforce data.

Powerful automation and scheduling capabilities are the major features of Cloudingo.

By running deduplication jobs in the background, teams spend fewer hours on repetitive data cleaning, allowing them to focus on higher-level tasks like analytics, customer engagement, and sales strategies.

This shift from manual work to automation can be a game-changer, especially for organizations that work with large or quickly growing data sets in Salesforce.

Specific scenarios where Cloudingo excels:

  1. If your marketing team imports leads from various events or external lists, you might get multiple versions of the same person. Cloudingo ensures those duplicates merge smoothly, so your sales reps don’t waste time calling the same lead twice.
  2. Maybe you’ve migrated customer data from an older CRM into Salesforce, resulting in duplicates or partial records. Cloudingo helps unify and standardize everything into a single, accurate set of data.
  3. Rapid Growth Organizations: If your company is scaling quickly, you might face an explosion of new contacts, leads, and accounts. Having an automated or even real-time process to keep data fresh and accurate can save hours of manual effort each week.

Customer Feedback About Cloudingo

Cloudingo has a 4.81 rating and 540+ reviews on AppExchange. After analyzing these reviews, we can make the following observations:

  • Many users say they were initially overwhelmed by the volume of duplicates but found that Salesforce Cloudingo handled the job effectively.
  • A significant number of users emphasize Cloudingo’s automation and scheduling features, which allow deduplication processes to run in the background, saving time and manual effort. Businesses report that setting up auto-merge rules has significantly reduced the need for hands-on data cleaning, allowing teams to focus on more strategic tasks.
  • Customer support is another standout feature in user reviews. Many users praise the responsiveness and expertise of the Cloudingo support team, with quick responses and effective solutions to issues. Several reviews mention that support agents take the time to help users understand complex filtering and merging scenarios.
  • Users also highlight Cloudingo’s ability to handle large-scale data management efficiently, stating that it has significantly reduced the time needed to merge thousands of records.
  • Some reviews specifically mention Cloudingo’s flexibility in handling custom objects and complex deduplication rules, which has made it an unreplaceable tool for organizations with complicated data structures.

Cloudingo pricing is publicly available and can be found on the AppExchange Cloudingo page and the official website.

Cloudingo license prices start at $2,500/year for a Standard license, $6,000/year for a Professional license, and $10,000/year for an Enterprise license. The license applies to the Salesforce Org and is billed annually. Discounts are available for nonprofits. A 10-day trial period is also available. Additionally, if a Salesforce Org contains more than 300,000 records in total between Leads, Contacts, Accounts, and Custom objects, the price increases by $100 for each additional 100,000 records.

Cloudingo stands out as a remarkably flexible and powerful Salesforce data cleansing tool. Its wide range of features, from real-time duplicate detection and scheduling to analytics and undo capabilities, make it easy for administrators to tackle everything from day-to-day cleanups to large-scale data migrations. By taking advantage of automation and user-defined matching rules, organizations can significantly reduce the hours spent on manual data merges, allowing teams to focus on more strategic tasks.

Moreover, Cloudingo’s user-friendly design, along with robust documentation and responsive customer support, underscores its commitment to a seamless experience. Whether you’re overseeing a small Salesforce org or an extensive enterprise with millions of records, Cloudingo’s customization options, detailed dashboards, and integration capabilities ensure that data remains trustworthy. Overall, Cloudingo stands out as a dedicated partner in keeping Salesforce environments clean and efficient, providing the kind of data integrity that every business strives for.

items.title: External Versioning in GCC: Defining Project Versions Without Modifying Source Code
items.author: Dimitrios Desyllas
items.content:
I was experimenting with gcc linker and how I could define the version of my project externally. The idea is to have a version bump procedure, but without needing to modify source code for just a version.

So assuming we have this file named :

#include <stdio.h>
#ifndef VERSION
#define VERSION "default"
#endif

int main(){
    printf(VERSION);
}

This is a piece of code that needs to output its version Instead of relying in a constant, we define the version with a placeholder.

The #define is a way to define macros, pieces of code that the preprocessor of the compiler will take and replace the values inside out actual code.

In our case, the preprocessor will replace the constant version with string . So once compiled, the piece of software above will just print if compiled like this:

gcc test.c
chmod +x ./a.out
./a.out
default

But as no noticed we did not just place a simple but instead we used . With that we tell the preprocessor to define the if not defined.

But is there a way to define it externally? YES babe YES we use the argument to define the desired version:

gcc -DVERSION="\"0.9.0\"" test.c
chmod +x ./a.out
./a.out

With the 's parameter we defined the constant externally resulting for the VERSION to be defined as and not as .

Therefore, we can use a file in which contains the version. Usually I name it VERSION. In our case it will contain:

Then I use a Makefile to build it:

VERSION := $(shell cat VERSION)
CC := go

.PHONY: all compile

# Default target
all: compile

# Compile Go binary
compile:
    gcc -DVERSION="\"$(VERSION)\"" test.c

So in order to compile I just run:

With this, I can version bump my project upon release. I just need to place the necessary version without needing to modify code, but instead I can set it upon compile time.

items.title: Dynamic routes in Next
items.author: udit saurabh
items.content:
*Dynamic routes in Next.js * Dynamic routes have something dynamic/changing about routing and paths. 

In our hotel management project, suppose we create a route blog section and wish to display blogs based on dynamic route change, like blog/1,blog/2, etc, then we can use this functionality.😁😁😁😁😁😁

We can do dynamic routing in just 2 simple steps.

Create a folder with the name blog (folder name along which we need to create the blog posts).

Create another folder for the dynamic route like in /blog/2 for 2 by the name of [id]. In the folder [id] we create a file for page.tsx for rendering the jsx content.

Code for extracting the dynamic route value for using it like id or slug.

export default async function Page({
    params,
  }: {
    params: Promise<{ id: string }>
  })  
{
    const {id} = await params
    return( <div>this is id {id}</div>)
}

items.title: Getting Started with Microservices: A Beginner's Guide Using Spring Boot
items.author: Abhijith
items.content:
Microservices have become an essential part of modern software architecture due to their flexibility, scalability, and ease of maintenance. In this blog, we will explore how to build microservices using Spring Boot. We will cover the integration of essential tools like for service discovery, for routing, for centralized configuration, and for distributed tracing.

By the end of this guide, you will have a working Spring Boot project with two microservices: and , running alongside an API Gateway, Eureka Discovery Server, Config Server, and Zipkin.

Before we begin, ensure you have the following:

  • Basic understanding of and .
  • Familiarity with concepts (Eureka, Config Server, etc.).
  • for dependency management.
  • (optional for Zipkin).

Overview of the Components

1. Microservices with Spring Boot

In this architecture, we have two microservices:

  • : Manages company-related data.
  • : Handles employee data.

Each microservice is a Spring Boot application that operates independently but interacts with other services via HTTP requests.

2. Eureka Discovery Server

Eureka provides service discovery. It allows microservices to register themselves and discover each other dynamically. By using Eureka, you eliminate the need to hard-code service URLs, enabling a more flexible system.

The is responsible for routing requests from clients to the appropriate microservices. It also offers additional features such as load balancing and security. In this demo, we will use for routing.

A centralizes the configuration for all microservices, making it easier to manage and update configurations without redeploying individual services.

5. Distributed Tracing with Zipkin

Distributed tracing helps track requests as they move through the various microservices. We'll use to visualize and trace requests across services. automatically integrates with Zipkin, providing trace and span information.

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Setting up Eureka Discovery Server

Start by creating a Spring Boot application for the .

1) Add the required dependencies in your :

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-netflix-eureka-server

2) Enable Eureka Server in your main application class:

3) Add Eureka configuration in application.yml:

Step 2: Creating Microservices

Both the Company Service and Employee Service will register with Eureka. Here's how to create them:

1) Add the following dependency to pom.xml for each microservice:

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-netflix-eureka-client

2) Enable Eureka Client in both microservices (Not Required for newer versions of Spring):

3) Configure application properties (application.yml):

Step 3: Setting up API Gateway

We'll use Spring Cloud Gateway to handle requests and route them to the appropriate microservices.

1) Add the required dependency for Spring Cloud Gateway:

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-gateway

2) Define routing in the application.yml:

This configuration ensures that the API Gateway routes requests to company-service and employee-service based on the request path.

Step 4: Config Server Setup

Create a new Spring Boot application for the Config Server.

1) Add the following dependencies:

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-config-serverorg.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-eureka

2) Enable Config Server in the main class:

3) Point the Config Server to a Git repository (or file system) that holds the configuration files for your microservices.

Step 5: Integrating Zipkin for Distributed Tracing

Add Zipkin dependencies to the employee, company, gateway microservices:

io.micrometermicrometer-tracing-bridge-braveio.zipkin.reporter2zipkin-reporter-brave

Configure Zipkin in application.yml:

Run Zipkin (via Docker or standalone) on port 9411. You can now trace requests across the microservices.

Once everything is set up, run the following services:

Eureka Server: localhost:8761 Company Service: localhost:8070

Employee Service: localhost:8090

API Gateway: localhost:8222

Config Server: localhost:8888 (optional if using a Config Server)

Access the API Gateway at http://localhost:8222 and make requests to /company and /employee. All requests will be routed to the appropriate microservices.

In this guide, we have successfully created 2 microservices with Spring Boot, integrating Eureka for service discovery, API Gateway for routing, Open Feign for communicating between the 2 microservices, Config Server for centralized configuration, and Zipkin for distributed tracing. These tools work together to help manage and monitor microservices effectively, providing a scalable and maintainable architecture.

With this setup, your microservices can scale independently, discover each other dynamically, and be monitored for performance and issues through distributed tracing.

You can access the full source code for this project on GitHub.

Happy coding with Spring Boot and Microservices!

items.title: Why Every Developer Needs a Portfolio (And Some Great Examples!)
items.author: pikes
items.content:
I wanted to talk about something that’s often overlooked but important — having a personal portfolio website. Whether you’re a software developer, security researcher, or even a designer, a portfolio isn’t just a “nice to have” — it’s your online identity and proof of work.

  • It’s Your Resume, but Better: Resumes list what you’ve done. A portfolio it.
  • Live demos, project write-ups, and case studies stand out way more than a bullet point.
  • Build Your Personal Brand: Your site reflects your style, creativity, and professionalism.
  • With the right SEO (and some backlinking), you can get noticed by recruiters and potential clients through Google search.
  • LinkedIn and GitHub are great, but your portfolio lets you tell your story the way want.

Some Inspiring Developer Portfolios:

  • Josh Comeau — Incredible animations and interactive content.
  • Waqar Naeem — My own portfolio! Software developer, security researcher, and freelancer. I’ve included my projects, blogs, and more. Would love feedback!
  • Cassie Evans — Great storytelling and creative use of SVG animations.
  • Chris Ferdinandi — A simple, content-focused approach with a wealth of knowledge.

Tips for Building Your Portfolio:

  • Highlight your best work and make navigation intuitive.
  • Add live demos and links to GitHub repositories.
  • Add an "About Me" Section: Share your journey, interests, and what you’re passionate about.
  • Meta tags, proper headings, and an XML sitemap can help your site rank on Google.
  • Make sure it looks great on all devices!

Would love to see some of your portfolios! Drop your links below and let’s inspire each other. 🚀
